segment-text: PARIS - MONTMARTRE - NARROW STREET - 10 YEARS AGO -
A YOUNG WOMAN, her back to us, tenderly places a small bouquet of flowers on the thin sidewalk.
NEW YORK - MURRAY HILL - PRESENT DAY An insipid brick apartment building. APARTMENT

A starter one-bedroom. The living room is a writer's workspace. The walls are BLANKETED in rejection letters from magazines and book publishers. We notice the particularly irritating profile of Eustace Tilley, unofficial mascot of The New Yorker magazine. He sits above that organ's form-letter rejection and seems to ooze disdain. At a black Formica parsons table, BRIAN, 25, hammers away at a computer keyboard -- hunt-and-peck style, but fairly fast. He has a good, kind, idealistic face.
When I think of what I wrote back then, you can't even call them tears -rivers of shame come out of my eyes.
EAST 55TH STREET - LUNCHTIME THAT DAY

Brian walks west toward 5th Avenue on the north side of 55th. He's lost in thought, doubtless about a story, when something catches his eye: Across the street, at the small smoking nook to the left of the entry stairs to the St. Regis Hotel, ARIELLE stands alone, smoking a Gitanes. She's 35, a European beauty. The sight of her snaps Brian to. He stops walking, stares. In his time in New York, he has seen a thousand beautiful women on the streets and has never spoken to one -- boldness is not his first instinct. But this time, something makes him go. As he crosses the street, he feels in his pocket for a pack of Marlboro Lights, reminds himself to breathe and to keep his shoulders back. What to say? What to say? Her gaze falls to him just as he reaches the sidewalk. Her eyes are light brown and dazzling, and his mind goes white. But there is no turning around now. He arrives at the nook and, silent at first, takes out a cigarette, lighting it with a Zippo. She's still looking at him. The inhale calms him. He smiles warmly, as always. He recognizes her brand of cigarette and wagers -- correctly -that she is French.
(in passable French, with SUBTITLES) We are exiles, the smokers.

She smiles at his absurd choice of opening, at his nervousness, at the courage it must have taken to overcome it, at the comically obvious reason why he did so, and at the instantaneous knowledge that French is not his first language. She likes him already. When she speaks, she is very confident but very kind. Her every movement is graceful, womanly, and sexy in a subtle, Parisian way. The following in French, with SUBTITLES:
I don't trust anyone who doesn't smoke.
Neither do I. They are far too fond of themselves.
Exactly. (then) Shall we continue in English?
(relieved) Please. And they do.
It's a Catch-22, though -- do you know this idiom? Catch-22?
Yes.
A Catch-22, because we are no longer allowed to smoke indoors anywhere, but smoking as you walk down the street is not really enjoyable, and maybe even a little crass.
Which leaves us here. like this.
"Nook." In a nook
Good word.
("I do speak the language, you know") I have lived here for ten years.
Still. I know people who have lived here all their lives and have never said "nook." Unless it was followed by "cranny."
"Cranny" I do not know. Seriously? "cranny"?
You know "nook" but not ARIELLE Yes.
That's extraordinary. That's like knowing "shocked" but not "appalled."
Or "oopsie" but not "daisy."
Pardon?
(regretting the riff) No, it's just, they go together.
What is "cranny"?
It's... (MORE)
(at a loss, suddenly) ...like a nook. But narrower.
The point is that smokers must band together.
Yes, because they are part of the same persecuted minority.
And they know this, which makes it easy to begin conversations.
Not that easy.
Relatively easy. There is instant common ground. A shared contrarian spirit. At this nook, I have had
several interesting conversations.
Is this one of them?
Yes.
(relieved) Good. (then) My name is Brian.
She laughs.
What?
That is the most American name I have ever heard.
It is?
No, I like it. It's just... ridiculously American.
Oh.
(attempts American accent) "Briannn." Yes.
May I ask your name?
(extends her hand) Arielle.
They shake hands.
Like "The Little Mermaid."
(scowls) What?
"The Little Mermaid." A Disney movie. The Little Mermaid's name was Ariel.
I don't know this word, "Mermaid."
Wow. I mean, you set the bar so high with "nook."
What is it?

(regretting this riff, too) Mermaid? Uh, it's a woman... a beautiful woman who lives in the sea and has... the tail of a fish. And yet she's... somehow for her, it all works. Mermaids are generally very confident and comfortable with themselves. Pure of heart and yet slightly mysterious. It seems.
(realizing) Ah, oui -- "sirene." Of course.
(testing it out) "Sirene."
(testing it out) "Mermaiddde."
Yes.
"Cranny."
Yes.
(enjoying him) Well. Enchantee, Briannn.
Enchantee.
She finishes her cigarette and puts it out in the nook's ashtray. He sneaks a look at the third finger on her left hand and discovers it ringless.
I must go back inside. To finish coffee with two non-smoking, entirely untrustworthy people.
I understand.
We will meet again?
The question catches him off guard. such forwardness. He is unaccustomed to
I would like it very much if that were to happen.
If it "were to happen"? It is not an act of G-d, Briannn. I am here every Friday at the close of lunch, with my Gitanes. You would simply have to be here as well.
Then, I will be. Good.
Until next Friday, then.
Until next Friday.

She smiles at him, turns, and mounts the stairs. The DOORMAN nods to her with familiarity and starts the revolving door. She steps in without looking back, and disappears into the hotel. Brian watches the door continue to spin.
(to himself) "The Little Mermaid." What is wrong with you? (then, happily, as he turns for the street) On the other hand, let us not bury the lead.
BRIAN'S BUILDING - MAILBOX AREA - THE NEXT DAY
Brian extracts from his mailbox business-sized envelopes from The Atlantic Monthly and Harpers.
BRIAN'S APARTMENT - MINUTES LATER

Brian adds the contents of those envelopes -- two rejection letters -- to the Ocean of No, as he speaks on his cellphone. The letter from the Atlantic Monthly bears the word "Sorry," handwritten in the corner.

(into phone) Work is going very well, Dad, thanks for asking. If nothing else, I think I've got a really interesting wallpaper design. Well, it's going to sound stupid, but the fiction editor from Atlantic Monthly handwrote "Sorry" in the corner of my latest rejection. Yes. It means I

got close. It's a code, Dad. I told you it was going to sound stupid. No -- thank you -- but I still have some left from the summer job. No -thank you -- but no law school. Please take the money you earmarked for tuition and spend it on yourself and Mom.
Brian turns on his computer.
Listen, I gotta go. I love you, too.
EXT. ST. REGIS HOTEL - THE FOLLOWING FRIDAY

It is RAINING SHEETS. Arielle, holding an umbrella, comes out the revolving doors and takes in the inclemency of the weather. Has it kept Brian away? Nope -- there he is, wet despite his raincoat and umbrella, smiling up at her from the smoking nook, freshly smitten but a little less nervous this time. She smiles back. She opens her umbrella and descends the stairs to join him. When she gets there, she kisses him on both cheeks. He is not expecting the second kiss.
I thought perhaps you wouldn't come.
It never occurred to me.
I mean, because of the rain.
(facetiously) Oh, is it raining?
(smiles) It was a good test. A woman has to know what sort of man she's dealing with. (then) What sort of man am I dealing with?
Damp. A damp sort of man. But happy in his... dampness. (then) What sort of woman am I dealing with?
(beat; smiles) A mermaiddde.
She takes out a cigarette. He takes out his Zippo and lights it before lighting his own.
Thank you.
They are close together under their commingled umbrellas.
So. Briannn. What is it you do in your life that leaves you free to wander across the street in the afternoons and smoke in nooks and talk to strangers?
(uncomfortable saying it) I write. I'm a writer.
Of...? Fiction.
Stories.
Have you published?
Not yet.
How old are you?
Well, then you have plenty of time. (then) Are you wondering how old I am?
Yes.
But you will not ask because it is impolite.
Correct.
I am 35.
(sincerely) You look younger than that.
Whereas you look 25, but seem older. You are an old soul.
May I ask your profession?
Are you asking me if I'm a prostitute?
What? No.
Did that also never occur to you? A woman you find attractive, alone in front of a hotel every Friday, who welcomes your advances?
(ashen beat) Are you a prostitute?
Certainly not. How dare you?
You brought it up. have -- I never would
She smiles -- she was teasing him.
I'm retired.
From prostitution?
He smiles -- he was teasing her.
I was a model. In Europe. moved here, I stopped.
You retired when you were 25?
It is a young woman's field. Besides, there were other things to do. (then) What are your stories about?
Uh, various things. One is about baseball. One is about dogs.
Dogs?
Yes.
You wrote a story about dogs?
Yes. Well, but, they're interesting dogs.
She nods and puts out her cigarette.
Back to coffee with your friends?
Yes.
Until next Friday?
If you wish. But, I am free any week night from 5 to 7.
Once again, he is taken aback by her casual boldness. he tries not to let on.
Those are very specific hours.
Not if you're Parisian. He scowls, not sure what she means, but sensing that it is best not to pursue the question. All right. at 5?
Shall we say... Monday
Do you like museums?
Yes.
Will you meet me at the Whitney?
I'd be delighted.
I want to see the Hoppers.
The Hoppers it shall be.
Good.
Till then, then.
She laughs, but affectionately, at the clumsy phrase.
Till then.
She smiles and mounts the stairs, the Doorman starting the revolving door, this time giving them both a nod of familiarity. When she's gone, he starts away, one part bewildered, nine parts delighted.
BRIAN'S APARTMENT - SATURDAY NIGHT
Rejection letters are added to the wall -- from The Saturday Evening Post and Esquire. Brian is on his cellphone, this time with his Mom.

(into phone) No, Mom, he didn't hand-write "We will publish your next story." Is that what Dad told you? He just wrote "Sorry." No, they didn't write anything on today's rejections. It's not a step backward. Progress is not linear. I don't know why they rejected them, Mom. They don't really say.
He turns on his computer.
Listen, I gotta go. I love you, too.
WHITNEY MUSEUM - THE FOLLOWING MONDAY - 5:15 PM

Brian and Arielle are looking at Hopper's "Early Sunday Morning," which, to Brian, looks like a perfectly nice picture of some perfectly appealing West Village houses on a perfectly lovely Sunday morning. He is smiling at them contentedly, until:
(conclusively) It is about death.
I beg your pardon? And menace. menace. It's houses. sunny.
It is about death and
Nice houses.
And it's
Is there any life on the street?
No.
And what is the opposite of life?
Death, but -ARIELLE It's like a neutron bomb went off.
Maybe everyone's just asleep.
And the menace! Look at that black tower in the background. A skyscraper. It is coming for those row houses.
The skyscraper is coming?
The massive urban bootprint is coming to annihilate those row houses. Those row houses are on death row.
I don't -ARIELLE And what is more American than a skyscraper? Nothing. The skyscraper is America. So logically, America is menace. America is death.
You know, this may be why there's friction between our two countries.
And it's not just this painting.
She is raising her voice, now, and other MUSEUM-GOERS are starting to notice. Arielle doesn't care, but Brian looks around apologetically.
Look at this one!
She moves to "7 AM," a seemingly innocuous portrait of a storefront with some woods behind.
Not a sign of life!
It's called "7 A.M." it's 7 AM.
Which means
And what do they sell in that store?
Um... Nothing!
It's a Mafia front.
Oh, for the love of G-d.
And those woods behind?
Let me guess: menace?
(louder still) Deep, dark, bottomless menace. She moves on to "Nighthawks," the famous picture of the urban diner at night.
And this one!
This one has people in it.
Dead people.
They're alive.
Not really. Look at that man? Is he alive? And the soda-jerk? Is that any kind of life? And the prostitute?
She's not a prostitute. What is it with you and prostitutes?
What is she, then?
She's, I don't know, she's a beautician.
They are all dead. And the store across the street is out of business. And no one lives in the apartment
upstairs. The tenants have been evicted and the building condemned. The people in the diner may be the
days are numbered. And they know it -you can see it in their faces. This Hopper is supposed to be the
Well if that's true, then America is not alive.
America is alive, okay? And you have a somewhat downbeat world-view. No I don't.
And, prove it.
Prove what?
That America is still alive.
(indicating other museum-goers) Look around.
(looking) They don't seem particularly alive to me. Prove it.
He scowls, then gets an idea.
All right.

And, suddenly, he takes her by the shoulders and kisses her. He's just as surprised as she is. It's a pretty long kiss, considering. Not too intimate, but definitely romantic. Other museum-goers are staring at them, now. When it finally ends:
(tossed off) You may be right -- I don't know.
INT. L'ABSINTHE RESTAURANT - EAST 67TH ST. - THE SAME EVENING - 6:10 PM
Brian and Arielle are at an outdoor table.
After ten years in New York I still cannot understand why there are not more outdoor cafes.
A crosstown bus BELCHES A CLOUD of exhaust fumes as it passes.
(coughs) Couldn't tell you.
I love it here, though. city most like Paris.
It is the
What made you come?
I married a diplomat, and he was assigned here.
I wondered if you had ever been married. What happened to him?
What do you mean?
The person you were married to.
Were married to? (then) Am married to. Nothing happened to him. He works at the French Consulate. It's eight blocks from here.
Brian is stunned. A moment, then:
S, uh, so you're... married? Of course.
What did you think?
I thought you were... not married.
Why would you think that?
Why would I not think that? I mean, for one thing, you don't wear a wedding ring. Even as she argues, there's a gentleness about it.
So American. You need a signpost for everything, or you completely lose your way.
It's not just American. Many cultures have... indicators of being married -rings, piercings, uh, dots -- designed to keep people out of exactly this sort of awkward situation.
Do you wish you were not in this situation?
(going past that one) Besides which, we... seemed to be mutually pursuing one another -ARIELLE So?
It's just that I would have thought that at some point you'd mention -ARIELLE Must everything be mentioned? Can nothing be culled from the subtext?
Well -ARIELLE Who would retire at 25 for any other reason besides marriage?
A model, you said.
My husband, like many Frenchmen, felt it was untoward for a diplomat's wife to be on international display in her underwear. And really I could not disagree.
No, well, sure.
Did you not understand the meaning of "5 to 7"?
Was my confusion about "5 to 7" unclear? I thought I was very clear about my confusion.
I thought you were kidding.
No, I was actually confused.
Oh. (then) Well. (then) The hours of 5 to 7 are traditionally reserved for mistresses and boyfriends.
Seriously? The French actually block out time for that?
Not the French, the Parisians. Please. We are as little like the rest of France as New Yorkers are like the rest of America.
The Parisians --
Not all of them, of course. some.
The amoral ones?
The old fashioned ones.
1 to 3, lunch, 3 to 5, conference call, 5 to 7, commit adultery -ARIELLE Look -BRIAN Are there set hours for breaking other commandments? Do you covet at 9:30? Worship false idols from 10 to noon?
She smiles, enjoying him.
(realizing) Do you have children? Two.
Would you like to see pictures?
It's all getting more stunning by the second. But Brian is always polite. And he really would like to see the pictures.
Yes.
She takes two small photos from her wallet.
Marc is 7, and Elodie is 5.
(looks at them; sincerely) They're beautiful children.
Thank you. They are the lights of my life. (then, looking at him) You are really thrown by this, aren't you?
(nods vigorously) I, yes, uh huh.
My husband has a mistress. One day I will stand next to her at his funeral.
Is that going to be... anytime soon?
In my culture, this is not judged so harshly.
Okay, but we're not in your culture. We're in my culture. And in my culture, if we didn't have things to judge harshly, we wouldn't know what to do all day.
Well, maybe your culture needs to grow. Maybe there are other ways to look at life. Maybe there are some people you marry and others you love.
(beat) It's very hard to win an argument with you.
Well, better you know that now.
He laughs.

Arielle. I have nothing but respect for your culture. The French have been French for much longer than the Americans have been American. I mean, a pre-War building in New York refers to World War II. In Paris, it's the Crusades. I get it. And I would never say that one way of life is any better than another. In addition to which, you're older than I am, and wiser, I'm sure. And you're beautiful and elegant and smart. And charming, and funny. And interesting and kind. And sexy and warm. And you feel like family, which is wonderful.
Are you seriously telling me that a "but" is coming at the end of this sentence?
Yes. "But"... naive and Puritanical and unsophisticated as it may be, being with you would be... an affair, and, to me, not ethical. Unethical. Not good, ethically.
A moment. And then, when she speaks, it's with the kindness that always flows beneath all her bluster and banter.
I must respect your ethics if I ask you to respect mine.
Thank you. And I'm sorry. I'm probably the stupidest man on the face of the earth.
You are not.
She puts the pictures away and makes ready to go.
Should you change your mind, I will continue to be at the smoking nook (MORE)
on Fridays. But if I never see you again, do know that I will always remember you very fondly. The honesty and openness with which their relationship is
ending is as shocking to Brian as was that with which it
And I you.
He looks at her. Her eyes are moist. This surprises him.
Are you okay? I'm sad.
It was the birth of love.

And when she says it, both know it's true. change his mind. Rules are rules. But he doesn't She rises, and then he does, too. She leans across the table and kisses him once on each cheek. He is expecting the second kiss this time. Then, she smiles and walks away, again without looking back. She turns the corner onto 3rd Avenue, and is gone.
BRIAN'S APARTMENT - A WEEK LATER

He is writing, but without much enthusiasm, energy or concentration. As if magnetized, his eyes drift repeatedly to the "day and date" at the corner of the computer screen. We see that it's Friday the 7th, toward the end of lunchtime. After a moment, he stops typing, his thoughts wandering. Then he forces them back and resumes working. EXT.
SMOKING NOOK - SAME TIME
Arielle is smoking alone, her eyes distant, her Gitanes dwindling down. She puts it out and goes up the hotel stairs.
BRIAN'S APARTMENT - A WEEK LATER

Today he is staring at the computer day and date -- Friday the 14th, lunchtime -- without lifting a finger to work. When he realizes he's doing this, he takes his head in his two hands and turns it back to the center of the screen. He types a listless word or two. EXT. SMOKING NOOK - SAME TIME Arielle smokes alone.
She seems not to be particularly
BRIAN'S APARTMENT - A WEEK LATER

Brian's chin rests on his hands, which are folded in front of the keyboard. Work is the farthest thing from his mind. His eyes flit to the day and date -- Friday the 21st, lunchtime -- and then, in a rush, he gets up.
(to himself) Fuckin' hell.
He grabs his jacket and is out the door.
EXT. SMOKING NOOK - A FEW MINUTES LATER

Arielle stands alone, taking out a Gitanes. She puts it to her lips and goes into her purse for a lighter, but A ZIPPO comes into frame, Brian's hand holding it. Her eyes widen in surprise as she realizes who's behind her. But she doesn't look at him right away. She takes in the flame, exhales the smoke, and then, suddenly and without a word, turns into his arms.
I can't believe it took you three weeks.
(laughs) Sorry. (then) What happens now?
She reaches into her purse and takes out
A ST. REGIS HOTEL KEY CARD
She presses it into his hand. Room 2117.
5 o'clock.
EXT. ST. REGIS HOTEL - 55TH STREET ENTRANCE - 5 O'CLOCK THAT EVENING
Brian, nervous, mounts the stairs. He has gone home, showered, shaved, changed, and probably changed again. doorman nods welcome and starts the revolving doors. INT.
ST. REGIS - LOBBY

It is surprisingly small, elegant but understated. The RECEPTIONISTS and CONCIERGES all have great personal style. Brian passes the entrance to the bar, moves through the sitting area, turns left at the front desk, and enters a waiting elevator. BENOIT, a uniformed, white-gloved elevator operator 50 years of age, is inside.
What floor, Monsieur?
21, please.
Benoit pushes the button for 21, and the elevator ascends. Brian stands there, awkward in the silence. After a few floors:
So how is the crowd today, wellbehaved?
Yes, Monsieur.
Very good.
More awkward silence, then:
That's great that they have you guys in the elevators. That's just a great touch.
Thank you, Monsieur. Mercifully, the elevator reaches 21.
Have a good evening.
The same to you, Monsieur.
Brian steps out into the swanky hallway and follows the directions -- engraved on a discreet brass plaque -- until he reaches Room 2117. He pauses at the door, then rings the
(sweetly, from within) Use your key.
He does. The light flashes green, and he enters.
ROOM 2117

It's a beautiful corner suite, with two exposures. Provincial, but under control. French Arielle is in the living room pouring two flutes of Mumm. Paolo Conte is SINGING quietly on the stereo -- "Via Con Me." The curtains are drawn so that the light is low. You can't hear the street. She looks at him as she pours, and smiles with her singular warmth.
How are you?
Very well, thank you. Very well. champagne? And you?
Will you have some
She brings it to him and looks him in the eye as they clink glasses.
Always look the person in the eye
when you touch glasses.
I know -- seven years of bad luck.
That is the American Bad luck?
Yes.
What's the French?
Seven years of bad sex.
Good lord.
Well, I think that tells you everything you need to know about our two cultures.
At any rate, best to look the other person in the eye.
Yes.
They sip the champagne.
Are you nervous?
No, I do this all the time.
She laughs.
I am.
What? Nervous.
I am nervous around you.
(beat) That's ridiculous.
Good nervous, I mean. girl.
Like a young
You don't show it.
No proper French girl ever would.
(beat) Sometimes, life is really something.
She scoffs.
What?
Maybe you should write fortune cookies.

He blushes, realizing how lame the line was. And then, her expression changes, and Brian sees that the flirting and joking are over for now. She takes him by the hand and walks into the bedroom. After they cross the threshold, she turns to him, her face searching his. For her, we see, this is no whim, no caprice. They kiss. They make love. It is tender and unguarded.
ROOM 2117 - BEDROOM - TWO HOURS LATER
They are naked, post-lovemaking, in the sheets.
I have to change careers, now.
What will you become?
A tourist.
She laughs. Then she looks at him, trying to read his
What?
(quietly) It was totally different.
From your expectations?
From the past.
Good. (then) And, just so you know, you are a natural lover. Your body expresses beautifully what is in your heart.
He looks at her, then reaches for the hotel pad on the nightstand.
I'm just going to write that down.
Have none of the other women you've been with told you as much?
Uh, no.
Well, they have thought it, I promise you.
She gets out of bed and starts to get dressed.
Do you have to go?
It is nearly 7. There's a fund-raiser at my children's school.
He nods, feeling the outside world re-assert itself.
Please don't take this the wrong way... but it suddenly seems impossible to me that you have that life.
I do not take it the wrong way. In fact, I feel the same. And will, until the moment I next see my children.
(beat) Will I see you tomorrow?
No. Monday. 5. The same room.
Hold onto the key.
Trust me.
She's dressed and ready to go.
She doesn't look the least
How do you do that?
What?
Look like that. In 30 seconds. Me, I'm going to have to walk through a car wash.
She smiles and warmly kisses him goodbye.
Say, "Till then, then," like you did last time.
"Till then, then."
(smiles) I like that.
She turns and goes. The door closes, and he's suddenly alone.
BRIAN'S APARTMENT - VERY LATE THAT NIGHT
He is pacing back and forth, smoking a cigarette with the windows open. On his computer screen is a SCREENSAVER -- a scroll that reads, "Brian Bloom is not working," and repeats at various angles.
I was way, way too happy to write.
EXT. PARIS THEATER - 58TH STREET - THE FOLLOWING MONDAY EVENING - 5 PM
Brian waits, hands in pockets, and then Arielle comes around the corner from 5th Avenue. They kiss twice on the cheek.
How are you?
I missed you. (then) Shall we go in?
He nods. As they do, we notice the theater's marquis: there is a 5:05 showing of a film with a French title.
INT. PARIS THEATER - HALF AN HOUR LATER
Brian and Arielle watch the dialogue-driven French-language film.
Are you reading the subtitles?
("I have to") These people speak fast.
One CHARACTER launches into a FRENZIED SPEECH. Arielle laughs happily as she listens. Brian squints at the screen; the SUBTITLES say only, "Where are my shoes?"
Okay, I know he said more than that.
Shh.
I'm just saying, I have a sense that I'm not getting the nuances.
Shhhh.

ON SCREEN, there is another SALVO OF WORDS. She laughs throughout, but then the SUBTITLES say only "They're in the hall closet." Brian turns his palms up -- "Are they kidding?" He shakes his head and eats some popcorn.
Can no American watch a film without popcorn?
That's right.
A vulgar tradition. How can films be taken seriously as art if you are sitting there chomping popcorn when you watch them? Do you chomp popcorn
when you read literature?
I eat sunflower seeds. And I spit the shells into a little cup.
(horrified) Do you chomp popcorn at a museum?
I would if I could.
Your culture has gone over the cliff.
(offering popcorn bucket) You know you want some.
(scoffs Frenchly) Pta.

He smiles to himself as they return their attention to the screen, where the fast-paced dialogue continues, along with the sorely inadequate subtitles. She resumes laughing. Her enjoyment so pleases him that he altogether stops caring whether he gets the nuances or not. He just watches her.
PARIS THEATER - LATER
They exit, arm in arm. She is still giggling.
...And the part where he admitted he had never tasted the cheese?! Wasn't that brilliant?
(nodding, smiling) No idea what you're talking about.
She laughs some more, stops, gazes at him, and then:
It's 7.
I know.
No time to go to the hotel.
I know.
You're not disappointed?

He looks at her. It's a moment of pure happiness, just standing in front of the Paris Theater on a clear New York evening with the person you are starting to love. PEDESTRIANS passing by, the poetry of the city all around you...
No.
There is always tomorrow.
Yes.
I cannot kiss you goodbye in public.
I understand.
People can see us together -- that is permitted. I can take your arm. But we cannot kiss.
If those are the rules, those are the rules.
And then, the following, in French, with SUBTITLES:
Do not doubt how much I want to.
Sometimes, that is enough.
He smiles and steps to the curb to hail her a taxi.
BRIAN'S APARTMENT - THE FOLLOWING DAWN

As little as you want to write when you're happy, that's how much you have to write when you're miserable. Your passions have to go somewhere, and this is the only place left. Your suffering has to be good for something. (beat) It's not for me to say if the words were worth the price.

Brian is walking north toward the Whitney Museum. We recognize him right away. The same good, kind, idealistic face. Maybe a drop less idealistic. But only a drop. He's with his family -- his WIFE and two young children, a TODDLING GIRL and a BABY BOY in a stroller. He's most affectionate with all three. His wife is five years younger than he. She's lovely. We can see immediately that the family is happy. Then, from BRIAN'S POV We subtly scan the faces of PEOPLE walking in the other direction. With so much time having gone by, he no longer sees Arielle in the face of every woman he passes. But it still happens from time to time. Especially when he goes anywhere near the places they visited together. Today, perhaps one out of every fifteen women he sees is, just for an instant, she. This no longer injures him, exactly; his expression tells us it's more of a warm, loving recall. But one out of fifteen is still one out of fifteen.
I wondered if it remained perfect for her as well, or if I was just being foolish.
They turn onto the Whitney's walkway and head toward the entrance.
I never thought I would have my answer. But one day, I did.

Valery, looking older but no less impressive, is with her, as are MARC and ELODIE, who are now well into their teens. Marc is wearing a baseball cap -- Brian's ancient influence. Arielle feels Brian's gaze and knows he is there before she sees him. She is already smiling when she brings her head around to him and meets his eyes. She looks the same. If there is any sign of age, he cannot see it. For an instant, there is once again nothing in the universe but the two of them. And then reality reasserts itself, as of course it must, as it always has. Valery now sees Brian. He smiles, but respectfully hangs back half a step. Brian's wife -- who, we strongly sense, has not been told of Arielle -- politely does the same. The two families come together. Arielle and Brian kiss on both cheeks. It, and everything that follows, is perfectly appropriate, honorable, gracious and respectful. Brian and Valery shake hands warmly. Marc and Elodie shake Brian's hand, both with beautiful manners. Arielle shakes Ms. Bloom's hand warmly, then marvels at the Bloom children. There is some chitchat. We HEAR snippets:

And then, too soon, it is time to go. But there is something Brian needs to know, and something Arielle needs to say, all without speaking. A bit more chatter...

He sees it. She sees him see it. Neither face betrays any reaction. And then she pulls her glove slowly back on. There are no exchanges of phone numbers that will never be dialed or email addresses that will never be used; there is no false ceremony. There are final goodbyes and achingly appropriate kisses, and then Arielle and Brian let their eyes meet for a last moment of connectedness, turning away at the same time, before anyone else has observed it. The Blooms head into the museum, and the Pierponts toward the sidewalk.
I don't know if I'll ever see her again. I don't know if that's a good thing or a bad one. (beat) But I will promise you this: your favorite story, whatever it might be, was written for one reader.

As she and her family turn north, Arielle steals a another final look in Brian's direction. He is gone; the door has closed. She turns away, keeps walking, and slowly disappears into the Sunday crowds. FADE OUT.
